event_text: Abel Tasman sights Aotearoa New Zealand, marking the first known European contact with the country.
James Cook maps much of the New Zealand coastline and European knowledge of the islands expands.
Sealers and whalers begin visiting New Zealand waters more frequently, increasing contact between Māori and Europeans.
Samuel Marsden preaches the first Christian service in New Zealand at Rangihoua in the Bay of Islands.
Mission stations, traders and shipping links increase contact between Māori communities and Europeans.
Musket warfare intensifies among Māori iwi and hapū, reshaping many communities before large-scale colonisation.
Thirteen northern rangatira ask King William IV for protection, reflecting growing concern over lawlessness and foreign influence.
The Declaration of Independence of New Zealand is signed by northern rangatira.
The New Zealand Association begins promoting organised British settlement.
The New Zealand Company sends settlers to establish Wellington, increasing pressure for British intervention.
William Hobson arrives in the Bay of Islands as Lieutenant-Governor.
Te Tiriti o Waitangi, the Treaty of Waitangi, is first signed at Waitangi.
British sovereignty over New Zealand is proclaimed.
Auckland is selected as the capital of the new colony.
New Zealand becomes a separate Crown colony, no longer part of New South Wales.
The Wairau Affray becomes one of the first serious armed clashes between Māori and settlers after the Treaty.
The Northern War begins in the Bay of Islands.
The Otago settlement is founded by Scottish settlers.
The Canterbury settlement begins, centred on Christchurch.
The New Zealand Constitution Act creates representative government and provincial councils.
New Zealand's first Parliament meets in Auckland.
Responsible government begins, giving elected ministers greater control over colonial affairs.
The first Māori King, Pōtatau Te Wherowhero, is chosen as leader of the Kīngitanga movement.
War begins in Taranaki over land purchase disputes and sovereignty.
Gold is discovered in Otago, transforming migration, settlement and the colonial economy.
The New Zealand Settlements Act allows land confiscations from Māori considered to be in rebellion.
Wellington becomes the capital of New Zealand.
Four Māori parliamentary seats are created.
Julius Vogel promotes large public works and immigration schemes to develop railways, roads and settlement.
The Vogel public works and immigration programme begins to transform transport, migration and colonial development.
The first national railway line opens between Christchurch and Ferrymead.
Provincial government is abolished, centralising political power in Wellington.
The Education Act establishes free, compulsory and secular primary education.
The first shipment of refrigerated meat leaves New Zealand, opening a major export trade with Britain.
Mount Tarawera erupts, destroying the Pink and White Terraces and killing more than 100 people.
The Liberal Party rises to power and begins a period of social and land reform.
New Zealand becomes the first self-governing country where women win the right to vote in parliamentary elections.
Women vote in a New Zealand general election for the first time.
Industrial arbitration law establishes a system for resolving employment disputes.
Old-age pensions are introduced, becoming a landmark in New Zealand social welfare.
New Zealand troops leave to fight in the South African War, also known as the Boer War.
New Zealand becomes a Dominion within the British Empire.
The population of New Zealand reaches about one million.
The Waihi miners' strike becomes one of the most serious industrial disputes in New Zealand history.
New Zealand enters the First World War after Britain declares war on Germany.
New Zealand forces occupy German Samoa during the First World War.
New Zealand and Australian troops land at Gallipoli.
The New Zealand Division captures Messines in Belgium during the First World War.
New Zealand suffers its worst military disaster at Passchendaele.
The First World War armistice is signed, ending the fighting on the Western Front.
The influenza pandemic reaches New Zealand and kills thousands of people.
The first Anzac Day commemorations are held in New Zealand.
New Zealand joins the League of Nations after the First World War.
The Balfour Declaration recognises the autonomy of Dominions including New Zealand.
The Hawke's Bay earthquake devastates Napier and Hastings.
The first Labour government is elected and begins major welfare and housing reforms.
The state housing programme begins to expand under the Labour government.
The Social Security Act creates a more comprehensive welfare system.
New Zealand enters the Second World War after Britain declares war on Germany.
The First Echelon of the Second New Zealand Expeditionary Force leaves for overseas service.
New Zealand troops take part in the Battle for Crete.
New Zealand troops suffer heavy casualties in the Italian campaign, including the battles around Cassino.
Victory in Europe is celebrated as the war in Europe ends.
Victory over Japan brings the Second World War to an end for New Zealand.
New Zealand signs the United Nations Charter.
New Zealand adopts the Statute of Westminster, confirming greater legislative independence.
The waterfront dispute becomes one of New Zealand's largest and most bitter industrial conflicts.
The Tangiwai railway disaster kills 151 people on Christmas Eve.
New Zealand joins the South East Asia Treaty Organisation during the Cold War.
New Zealand's last death sentence is carried out, ahead of the abolition of capital punishment for murder.
Regular television broadcasting begins in New Zealand.
New Zealand combat troops are sent to the Vietnam War.
The inter-island ferry Wahine sinks in Wellington Harbour during a storm.
The voting age is lowered from 21 to 20.
The voting age is lowered to 18.
The Māori language petition is presented to Parliament, helping drive the revival of te reo Māori.
Britain joins the European Economic Community, forcing New Zealand to diversify its trade relationships.
New Zealand introduces a domestic purposes benefit, changing support for sole parents.
Dame Whina Cooper leads the Māori Land March to Parliament.
The Waitangi Tribunal is established to investigate breaches of the Treaty of Waitangi.
The Accident Compensation Corporation begins operating, introducing a no-fault accident compensation system.
Air New Zealand Flight TE901 crashes into Mount Erebus in Antarctica.
The Springbok rugby tour divides New Zealand and sparks major protests over apartheid.
The Fourth Labour Government is elected and begins sweeping economic reforms.
French agents bomb the Greenpeace ship Rainbow Warrior in Auckland Harbour.
The Homosexual Law Reform Act decriminalises sex between men aged 16 and over.
The Māori Language Act makes te reo Māori an official language of New Zealand.
New Zealand becomes nuclear-free through the New Zealand Nuclear Free Zone, Disarmament, and Arms Control Act.
Major local government reforms reshape councils and regional authorities.
The sesquicentennial of the Treaty of Waitangi prompts reflection on national identity and Treaty relationships.
The Resource Management Act becomes New Zealand's central environmental planning law.
New Zealand votes to adopt Mixed Member Proportional representation.
New Zealand holds its first general election under the MMP voting system.
Jenny Shipley becomes New Zealand's first woman Prime Minister.
Helen Clark becomes Prime Minister after the general election.
The Foreshore and Seabed Act becomes a major national debate over Māori customary rights and the coast.
John Key becomes Prime Minister after the general election.
A major earthquake strikes Canterbury and begins a period of severe seismic disruption.
The Christchurch earthquake kills 185 people and causes major destruction in the city.
The Rugby World Cup is hosted in New Zealand and the All Blacks win the tournament.
The Marriage Amendment Act legalises same-sex marriage in New Zealand.
A national flag referendum process begins, prompting debate about symbols of national identity.
The Kaikōura earthquake damages transport links and reshapes parts of the coast.
Jacinda Ardern becomes Prime Minister after the general election.
A terrorist attack on two Christchurch mosques kills 51 people.
New Zealand enters a nationwide COVID-19 lockdown under Alert Level 4.
The Labour Party wins a majority in the general election held during the COVID-19 pandemic.
Matariki is celebrated as a national public holiday for the first time.
Te Pae Mahara, the Māori Crown Relations framework, continues to shape Treaty settlement and Crown engagement work.
Severe flooding affects Auckland and other parts of the North Island.
Cyclone Gabrielle causes widespread damage across the North Island and becomes one of New Zealand's costliest natural disasters.
New Zealand holds a general election that leads to a change of government.
Christopher Luxon becomes Prime Minister following the 2023 general election.
